content.text: Why CODEOWNERS Matters for Claude Application Repos

In a conventional codebase, the worst outcome of an unreviewed change is a broken feature. In a Claude application repo, an unreviewed change to a system prompt or tool schema can alter the model's behaviour for every user, potentially violating safety guarantees, breaking integrations, or producing outputs that were explicitly prohibited in the behaviour specification.

CODEOWNERS enforces that the right people review changes to the right files — automatically, on every pull request. For AI application repos, the critical files are the prompt registry, tool definitions, and evaluation suite. Without CODEOWNERS, these files are reviewed with the same scrutiny as a CSS change: whoever happens to be reviewing the PR at the time.

This sub-subtopic defines the CODEOWNERS structure for Claude repos, which teams own which paths, how to combine CODEOWNERS with branch protection to make reviews mandatory, how to audit CODEOWNERS coverage to detect gaps, and how to write tests that catch ownership violations before they reach a production merge.
CODEOWNERS Coverage Map

attrs.alt: CODEOWNERS coverage map: ai-safety-team owns prompts/, api-team owns tools/, eval-team owns evals/, agent-team owns src/agents/

content.text: The four ownership zones in a Claude application repo:
Path Pattern
Owning Team
What They Guard
prompts/**
@ai-safety-team

All system prompts, user prompt templates, and the prompt hash registry. Any change to these files requires at least one member of the AI safety team to review the diff and confirm the change is safe to deploy.
tools/**
@api-team
Tool JSON schemas — the function definitions that tell Claude what tools are available and what arguments they accept. The API team owns the interface contract; callers depend on schema stability.
evals/**
@eval-team
Evaluation case files, golden outputs, scoring threshold configs, and harness scripts. The eval team owns the quality bar.
src/agents/**
@agent-team
Orchestration code: routing logic, multi-step planner, agent-to-agent communication. Separate from prompt ownership because agent logic changes can affect behaviour without touching any prompt file.
docs/behaviour-spec.md
@ai-safety-team @product-owners

The behaviour specification that defines what the product should and should not do. Requires consensus from both safety and product because changes to the spec define the boundary between acceptable and unacceptable AI behaviour.
.releaserc.json .github/workflows/release.yml
@platform-team
Release infrastructure. Protects against accidentally disabling the release pipeline or removing required CI gates.
The CODEOWNERS File
Create the file at .github/CODEOWNERS. Rules are evaluated top-to-bottom and the last matching rule wins, so more specific patterns should come after more general ones:

# .github/CODEOWNERS
# Default owner for everything not explicitly covered below.
# This is a catch-all so that no file is ever unowned.
*  @platform-team

# Prompt registry and all system/user prompt files.
# @ai-safety-team must approve every prompt change before merge.
prompts/                    @ai-safety-team
prompts/**/*.json           @ai-safety-team

# Tool schema definitions.
# @api-team owns the interface contract with callers.
tools/                      @api-team
tools/**/*.json             @api-team

# Evaluation suite: cases, golden outputs, thresholds, harness.
evals/                      @eval-team
evals/**                    @eval-team

# Agent orchestration — routing, planning, memory management.
src/agents/                 @agent-team
src/agents/**               @agent-team

# Behaviour specification: both safety and product must agree on changes.
docs/behaviour-spec.md      @ai-safety-team @product-owners

# Release infrastructure — changes must not remove required CI gates.
.releaserc.json             @platform-team
.github/workflows/          @platform-team

# This CODEOWNERS file itself.
.github/CODEOWNERS          @ai-safety-team @platform-team

Two patterns per directory (e.g., prompts/ and prompts/**/*.json) are intentional: the directory pattern covers new files added without a suffix match, while the glob pattern covers specific file types. Without both, a new file at an unexpected path could slip through without triggering an owner review.
Making Reviews Mandatory via Branch Protection
CODEOWNERS defines ownership, but it only triggers mandatory reviews when combined with the correct branch protection settings. There are two settings that must both be enabled:
Require pull request reviews before merging
: set to require at least 1 approval.
Require review from Code Owners
: this is the critical one. Without this, a PR that touches prompts/ will request a review from @ai-safety-team but will not block the merge if that review is skipped.
Configure via the GitHub API:

# Enable required code owner reviews on the main branch.
# The 'require_code_owner_reviews: true' field is the critical setting.
gh api repos/{owner}/{repo}/branches/main/protection \
  --method PUT \
  --field required_pull_request_reviews='{"required_approving_review_count":1,"require_code_owner_reviews":true,"dismiss_stale_reviews":true}' \
  --field enforce_admins=true \
  --field required_status_checks='{"strict":true,"contexts":["verify-eval-refs","lint","unit-tests"]}' \
  --field restrictions=null
Or via Terraform if the repository is managed as infrastructure:
Auditing CODEOWNERS Coverage

Over time, new directories and file types are added to the repo that are not covered by CODEOWNERS. A coverage audit script checks every tracked file against the CODEOWNERS rules and reports any that fall through to the catch-all or have no owner at all:

# scripts/audit_codeowners.py
"""
Audit CODEOWNERS coverage across the repository.

Identifies files that:
1. Have no matching rule (will not trigger any review).
2. Fall through to the catch-all * rule (reviewed generically, not by
   the team with domain knowledge).
3. Are in sensitive directories (prompts/, tools/, evals/) but are
   matched by a non-specific rule.

Usage:
    python scripts/audit_codeowners.py [--fail-on-gaps]

Exits with code 1 if --fail-on-gaps is set and unowned files are found.
"""
import subprocess, re, sys
from pathlib import Path

# Sensitive paths — any file here MUST be matched by a specific rule,
# not the catch-all *.
SENSITIVE_PREFIXES = ("prompts/", "tools/", "evals/")

def load_codeowners(codeowners_path: Path) -> list[tuple[str, list[str]]]:
    """
    Parse CODEOWNERS into a list of (pattern, owners) tuples.
    Comments and blank lines are ignored.
    Returns rules in order (last match wins in GitHub's implementation).
    """
    rules = []
    for line in codeowners_path.read_text().splitlines():
        line = line.strip()
        if not line or line.startswith("#"):
            continue
        parts = line.split()
        pattern = parts[0]
        owners = parts[1:]
        rules.append((pattern, owners))
    return rules

def pattern_matches(pattern: str, filepath: str) -> bool:
    """
    Approximate GitHub CODEOWNERS pattern matching.
    Handles:
      - Exact file match (no wildcards)
      - Directory match (trailing /)
      - Glob patterns (* and **)
    """
    import fnmatch
    # Trailing slash → match anything inside the directory
    if pattern.endswith("/"):
        return filepath.startswith(pattern) or filepath.startswith(pattern.rstrip("/") + "/")
    # ** → any depth
    if "**" in pattern:
        regex = pattern.replace(".", "\\.").replace("**", ".*").replace("*", "[^/]*")
        return bool(re.match(f"^{regex}$", filepath))
    # * → any single path segment component
    if "*" in pattern:
        return fnmatch.fnmatch(filepath, pattern)
    # Exact match
    return filepath == pattern or filepath.startswith(pattern + "/")

def find_owner(filepath: str, rules: list[tuple[str, list[str]]]) -> tuple[str, list[str]] | None:
    """
    Return the (pattern, owners) for the last matching rule.
    Returns None if no rule matches (should not happen if * is defined).
    """
    matched = None
    for pattern, owners in rules:
        if pattern_matches(pattern, filepath):
            matched = (pattern, owners)
    return matched

def get_all_tracked_files() -> list[str]:
    """Return all files tracked by git, relative to the repo root."""
    result = subprocess.run(
        ["git", "ls-files"],
        capture_output=True, text=True, check=True,
    )
    return result.stdout.strip().splitlines()

def audit(fail_on_gaps: bool = False) -> None:
    """
    Run the CODEOWNERS audit and print a report.
    Exits with code 1 if fail_on_gaps is True and issues are found.
    """
    repo_root = Path(
        subprocess.run(
            ["git", "rev-parse", "--show-toplevel"],
            capture_output=True, text=True, check=True,
        ).stdout.strip()
    )
    codeowners_path = repo_root / ".github" / "CODEOWNERS"
    rules = load_codeowners(codeowners_path)
    files = get_all_tracked_files()

    unowned = []       # no matching rule at all
    catchall_only = [] # matched only by the * rule
    sensitive_catchall = []  # sensitive file matched only by *

    for filepath in files:
        match = find_owner(filepath, rules)
        if match is None:
            unowned.append(filepath)
            continue
        pattern, owners = match
        if pattern == "*":
            catchall_only.append(filepath)
            if any(filepath.startswith(p) for p in SENSITIVE_PREFIXES):
                sensitive_catchall.append(filepath)

    issues = 0

    if unowned:
        print(f"ERROR: {len(unowned)} files with no CODEOWNERS match:")
        for f in unowned[:10]:
            print(f"  {f}")
        issues += len(unowned)

    if sensitive_catchall:
        print(f"\nERROR: {len(sensitive_catchall)} sensitive files matched only by catch-all (*):")
        for f in sensitive_catchall[:10]:
            print(f"  {f}")
        issues += len(sensitive_catchall)

    if catchall_only:
        print(f"\nINFO: {len(catchall_only)} files matched only by catch-all (*) — consider adding specific rules")

    if issues == 0:
        print(f"OK: all {len(files)} tracked files have specific CODEOWNERS coverage")

    if fail_on_gaps and issues > 0:
        sys.exit(1)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    fail_on_gaps = "--fail-on-gaps" in sys.argv
    audit(fail_on_gaps=fail_on_gaps)

# .github/workflows/codeowners-audit.yml
name: CODEOWNERS audit

on:
  schedule:
    - cron: '0 6 * * 1'  # weekly, Monday morning
  pull_request:
    paths:
      - '.github/CODEOWNERS'
      - 'prompts/**'
      - 'tools/**'
      - 'evals/**'

jobs:
  audit:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version: '3.12'
      - name: Audit CODEOWNERS coverage
        run: python scripts/audit_codeowners.py --fail-on-gaps
Common CODEOWNERS Mistakes in Claude Repos
Mistake
Consequence
Correct Pattern
Owning the directory but not subdirectories
New agents created in subdirectories are unowned
Use prompts/** not just prompts/
Listing individuals instead of teams
When the individual leaves, the rule still matches but nobody is notified
Always use @team-slug, never @personal-handle for production files
No catch-all rule
Files not explicitly listed have no owner and no required review
Always include * @catch-all-team as the first rule
CODEOWNERS without require_code_owner_reviews
Reviews are requested but not mandatory — PRs can merge without them
Enable require_code_owner_reviews: true in branch protection
Putting CODEOWNERS in the root instead of .github/
GitHub supports root or docs/ as alternatives, but .github/ is the standard — mixing locations confuses tooling
Always use .github/CODEOWNERS
Not listing the CODEOWNERS file itself as owned
Anyone can modify the ownership rules without triggering a review
Add .github/CODEOWNERS @ai-safety-team @platform-team
Testing CODEOWNERS Coverage in CI

# tests/test_codeowners_coverage.py
"""
Tests that verify CODEOWNERS covers the files that matter most
in a Claude application repo.
"""
import unittest
from pathlib import Path
from scripts.audit_codeowners import load_codeowners, find_owner, pattern_matches

REPO_ROOT = Path(__file__).parent.parent
CODEOWNERS = REPO_ROOT / ".github" / "CODEOWNERS"

class TestPatternMatching(unittest.TestCase):

    def test_directory_pattern_matches_subdirectory(self):
        self.assertTrue(pattern_matches("prompts/", "prompts/triage-agent/system_prompt.json"))

    def test_double_glob_matches_nested_file(self):
        self.assertTrue(pattern_matches("prompts/**", "prompts/triage-agent/system_prompt.json"))

    def test_exact_file_pattern(self):
        self.assertTrue(pattern_matches("docs/behaviour-spec.md", "docs/behaviour-spec.md"))

    def test_no_match_outside_directory(self):
        self.assertFalse(pattern_matches("prompts/", "src/agents/router.py"))

class TestCodeownersFile(unittest.TestCase):
    """
    Tests that run against the actual .github/CODEOWNERS file in the repo.
    These tests verify that sensitive paths have the expected owners.
    """

    @classmethod
    def setUpClass(cls):
        cls.rules = load_codeowners(CODEOWNERS)

    def _get_owners(self, filepath: str) -> list[str]:
        match = find_owner(filepath, self.rules)
        if match is None:
            return []
        _, owners = match
        return owners

    def test_system_prompt_owned_by_ai_safety(self):
        owners = self._get_owners("prompts/triage-agent/system_prompt.json")
        self.assertIn("@ai-safety-team", owners,
            "System prompt files must be owned by @ai-safety-team")

    def test_tool_schema_owned_by_api_team(self):
        owners = self._get_owners("tools/citation_tool.json")
        self.assertIn("@api-team", owners,
            "Tool schema files must be owned by @api-team")

    def test_eval_case_owned_by_eval_team(self):
        owners = self._get_owners("evals/triage/refusal-v4.json")
        self.assertIn("@eval-team", owners,
            "Eval case files must be owned by @eval-team")

    def test_behaviour_spec_owned_by_both_safety_and_product(self):
        owners = self._get_owners("docs/behaviour-spec.md")
        self.assertIn("@ai-safety-team", owners)
        self.assertIn("@product-owners", owners)

    def test_codeowners_file_itself_has_owner(self):
        owners = self._get_owners(".github/CODEOWNERS")
        self.assertTrue(len(owners) > 0,
            "CODEOWNERS must be owned to prevent unreviewed rule changes")

    def test_no_sensitive_file_falls_to_catchall_only(self):
        sensitive = [
            "prompts/triage-agent/system_prompt.json",
            "tools/citation_tool.json",
            "evals/triage/refusal-v4.json",
        ]
        for path in sensitive:
            match = find_owner(path, self.rules)
            self.assertIsNotNone(match, f"{path} has no CODEOWNERS match")
            pattern, _ = match
            self.assertNotEqual(pattern, "*",
                f"{path} falls through to the catch-all — add a specific rule")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    unittest.main()
Maintaining CODEOWNERS as the Repo Grows
A CODEOWNERS file becomes stale when new directories are added without corresponding ownership rules. Three practices keep it accurate over time:
Run the coverage audit on PRs that add new directories

. The audit workflow above triggers on changes to .github/CODEOWNERS, prompts/, tools/, and evals/ — meaning a PR that adds a new agent directory will fail the audit if no ownership rule covers the new path.
Review CODEOWNERS in new-agent onboarding checklists
. When a new agent is added to the repo, the checklist should include a step to add ownership rules for prompts/<agent-name>/ and optionally evals/<agent-name>/.
Quarterly ownership review

. Schedule a quarterly review where team leads verify that the team mappings in CODEOWNERS still reflect actual team boundaries. Teams are renamed, merged, and split — the CODEOWNERS file does not update itself.
Summary

CODEOWNERS for a Claude application repo is a safety control, not just an organisational convenience. By assigning @ai-safety-team to prompts/, @api-team to tools/, and @eval-team to evals/, the file ensures that every change to the AI layer is reviewed by someone with domain knowledge of what that layer does and what can go wrong. The ownership rules only become mandatory when branch protection enables require_code_owner_reviews: true. Regular coverage audits, CODEOWNERS unit tests, and new-agent onboarding checklists keep the file accurate as the repository grows.
